content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 2, 1990

William A. Duval
International Brotherhood of
Painters and Allied Trades
1750 New York Avenue, NW
Washington, D.C. 20008

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-115

Dear Mr. Duval:

You have requested advice on behalf of the International
Brotherhood of Painters and Allied Trades concerning the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1

QUESTION

What reporting obligations does the International Brotherhood
of Painters and Allied Trades (PAT) have in California if it
contributes to California state or local candidates or committees?

DISCUSSION

In order to obtain additional information, I called your
office on February 26, 1990, and again on March 7, 1990. Frank
Burkhardt of your office returned my call on March 19, 1990.
Unfortunately, he was unable to provide the information necessary
to answer your question accurately. He advised that I respond to
your letter by establishing the different scenarios under which
PAT may have reporting obligations.

SCENARIO I - Assume that PAT has not made contributions2 in
any amount to California state or local candidates or committees
prior to January 1, 1990. Also assume that PAT has made or

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 A contribution is defined by Section 82015 in part as "a
payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third
party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment." The
definition includes non-monetary contributions and loans.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

William A. Duval
Page Two

intends to make contributions to California state and local
candidates and committees totaling less than $10,000 in 1990.

Under Scenario I, PAT would have no filing obligations in
California for the calendar year 1990. (Section 82013.)

(Note: If PAT makes any contribution to state or local candidates
or committees in California in 1990, PAT will have reporting
obligations in any subsequent year in which PAT contributes $1,000
or more. (Section 82013(a).)

SCENARIO II - Assume that PAT has not made contributions in
any amount to California state or local candidates or committees
prior to January 1, 1990. Also assume that PAT has made or
intends to make contributions in California totaling $10,000 or
more in 1990.

Under Scenario II, PAT will have a reporting obligation once
it has contributed $10,000. (Section 82013(c).) Enclosed is the
Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the
Political Reform Act. Organizations, such as yours, which have
not made contributions in California in previous years but have
contributed $10,000 or more in the current calendar year are
considered "Major Donor Committees." Please refer to page 10 of
the enclosed manual for a discussion on the three different types
of major donor committees, and review pages 21 and 22 to determine
when reports must be filed in 1990.

As a brief example, if your organization contributes $10,000
or more prior to June 30, 1990, to state candidates or committees,
or candidates and committees in more than one county, PAT must
file the enclosed Form 461 by July 31, 1990. (Please note that
PAT's filing obligations in 1991 and subsequent years are the same
as would apply under Scenario III, below.)

SCENARIO III - Assume PAT has made contributions to
California state or local candidates or committees prior to
January 1, 1990. Also assume that PAT intends to make
contributions totaling $1,000 this calendar year.

Under Scenario III, PAT must file the enclosed Form 410 with
the Secretary of State's office within 10 days of contributing
$1,000 in California. (Section 84101.) The Secretary of State's
address is at the top of the form. Upon filing the Form 410, PAT
will be a "general purpose recipient committee." (Section 84101.)

This committee must report its activities in California.
(Section 84211.) Most labor unions are able to use the enclosed
Form 450 to report their activities. The reporting periods and
filing schedule for 1990 is included in the enclosed pamphlet

William A. Duval
Page Three

entitled "Filing Schedules for Elections Held in 1990." In
addition, the enclosed document entitled "Organizations Which
Receive Funds for Purposes Other than Making Contributions to or
Expenditures on Behalf of California State or Local Candidates,
Ballot Measures, or Committees" will assist you in reporting.

If you have any additional questions, please call me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures
